title: Chapter 18: The Wedding Planner
content: The basket arrived at half past ten on a Tuesday, carried by Edward's assistant with the same expressionless efficiency one might use to deliver a quarterly earnings report. Crisp white cellophane. A satin bow in Thornfield charcoal. The assistant — Jane never learned her name, only her flat affect and her sensible shoes — set it on the console table outside Blanche's dressing room and walked away without a word.

Blanche found it twenty minutes later. She told people it was a gift. A congratulations, perhaps, for the winter campaign she'd closed — the one that had plastered her face across six European cities, lips parted, throat bare, wearing nothing but a Thornfield coat slouched off one shoulder. *Unfasten your expectations.* That had been the tagline. Edward had written it himself.

She opened the basket in her dressing room. She read the letter. And then she sat very still for a long time, holding a single sheet of Thornfield-branded stationery in hands that had stopped shaking only because her whole body had gone rigid with the particular numbness that precedes catastrophe.
*Dear Blanche,*
*Your ambassadorship is concluded. Thank you for your invaluable contribution to the Thornfield brand. We wish you every success in your future endeavors.*
*With gratitude,*
*Edward Rochester*
*CEO, Thornfield Maison*

We. He'd written "we," as though the entire house had gathered to vote on her eviction. As though it were a corporate decision arrived at by committee, with minutes taken and objectives reviewed. Not the private act of a man removing a woman from his life like a stain from silk.

The basket itself was cruel in its thoughtfulness. A bottle of Sancerre — her preferred vintage. A box of diptyque candles — fig, the scent she always requested for her hotel rooms. And nestled in tissue paper at the center, wrapped with the care usually reserved for engagement rings, a scarf. Thornfield's signature jacquard, last season's colorway, the one that had already been discountedin the Paris outlet. Beautifully presented. Utterly surplus. A severance package in cashmere.

Blanche pressed the scarf to her face and breathed in. It smelled of cedar — the Indian sandalwood finish Thornfield used on all their wool goods — and underneath it, something synthetic, something factory-sharp, the chemical ghost of a textile mill in Lyon. It smelled like product. Like inventory. Like herself.
Jane found her by accident.

She'd been looking for a replacement bobbin for the sample loom — one of the antique Lucbourg units Edward kept in the atelier's west wing, more decorative than functional, but Jane had taken to using it for initial prototyping. The supply closet was two doors past Blanche's dressing room, down a corridor that smelled of starch and the faintest trace of turpentine from the photography studio.

She heard the sound before she saw the source. Not delicate crying — not the crystalline, cinematic grief of a woman in a Thornfield campaign. This was something else. Full-body, snotted, heaving sobs that came from somewhere deep in the chest, the kind of crying that rearranges your face and leaves you unrecognizable even to yourself.
Jane opened the door.

Blanche Ingram sat on the floor between a rack of muslin backing and a shelf of thread cones, her knees pulled up, her mascara tracking down her cheeks in twin rivers of black. She was still wearing Thornfield — all of it, head to toe, the winter collection she'd helped launch, the camel cashmere coat and the silk charmeuse blouse and the wool trousers that fit her like a second skin because Edward had personally overseen every fitting. She looked like a mannequin that had been pushed off its pedestal and couldn't figure out how to stand back up.

The gift basket sat in her lap. She was clutching it to her chest with both arms, the cellophane crinkling against the silk of her blouse with each shuddering breath. The sound was obscene — that cheap, scratchy crackle of packaging against something fine.
"Blanche." Jane said her name quietly, the way you'd approach an animal you didn't want to startle.
"Go away." Blanche's voice was hoarse, stripped raw. "Go away, you don't need to — this isn't —"

But she didn't look up, and she didn't let go of the basket, and her body kept shaking with those terrible, uncontrolled sobs that seemed to have no end in sight, just wave after wave breaking against the same shore.

Jane stepped inside and closed the door behind her. The closet was small, warm, close. The air tasted of fabric softener and something else — salt, maybe, or the metallic tang of cosmetics crying themselves off a face. She lowered herself to the floor across from Blanche, her back against the opposite shelf, and waited.

It took four minutes. Jane counted. Four minutes of watching the woman who had been her rival — the woman who had stood in the Thornfield atelier with Edward's hand on her waist and the whole room arranged around her like a solar system — dissolve into this: a person on a supply closet floor, wearing thousands of euros of clothing, holding a gift basket like a life raft in a sea she couldn't navigate.
When Blanche finally looked up, her eyes were swollen almost shut. The mascara had reached her jawline. She looked wrecked. She looked real.

"He fired me," Blanche said. The word *fired* came out strange in her mouth — too blue-collar for a woman who'd never thought of herself as an employee. "Through his assistant. He couldn't even do it himself." A wet laugh. "That would require courage."

Jane said nothing. She'd learned, in her months at Thornfield, that silence was the most useful tool in any room. People filled it. They told you things they hadn't planned to say. They revealed the architecture of their own deception.

"He told me I was his muse." Blanche's voice cracked on *muse*, splitting the word open like a seam under tension. "Three years. Three *years* I wore his clothes, laughed at his jokes, let him reshape my entire aesthetic around what he wanted to see. I used to wear color, did you know that? Bright things. Magenta. Turquoise. I had *taste* before I had a brand." She wiped her nose with the back of her hand, smearing mascara across her cheekbone. "Now I open my closet and everything is charcoal and camel and navy. He hollowed me out and called it curation."

Jane felt the recognition move through her like a current. *He told me I was unformed,* she thought. *That he'd refine me.* She hadn't said it aloud to anyone — hadn't even articulated it fully to herself — but the words were there, waiting, precise as a pattern piece.
"He told me I was unformed," Jane said. "That he'd refine me."

Blanche stopped crying. Not gradually — all at once, like a faucet shutting off. She stared at Jane, and something shifted in her expression. Not sympathy. Something worse. Recognition of the shared mechanism, the identical machinery of control dressed up as devotion.
"Then you're the next me." Blanche's voice was low, flattened by the weight of the observation. "Unless you're the next her."
She nodded downward. Toward the floor. Below them. Below the atelier and the showroom and the polished concrete and the carefully curated spaces where Thornfield's public life unfolded. Below.
Jane's throat tightened. "You know about —"

"I don't know *about* anything." Blanche's voice was sharp now, defensive, the voice of a woman defending a wall she'd built from willful ignorance. "I heard sounds. Late at night. The scissors — that snipping, like someone cutting and cutting and cutting. The crying. This little broken crying that would start and stop and start again. I told myself it was the heating system." She took a breath that shuddered through her entire body. "Because if it was a person, then what he was doing to me wasn't love."

The supply closet was very quiet. The muslin on the rack above Blanche's head stirred slightly, though there was no draft. Jane could hear the distant clatter of the atelier — the steady rhythm of the industrial machines, a designer arguing about a hemline, the ordinary music of a fashion house in production. None of it reached this closet. Here, there was only the sound of two women breathing.
"It was never love," Blanche said. "It was ownership. And I let him because I thought ownership was the same thing."

She looked down at the basket in her lap. Ran her thumb along the satin ribbon. A gesture so practiced, so trained — touching luxurious things as though they were extensions of her own body — that it seemed involuntary, the muscle memory of three years of being ornamental.

"You could come out of this closet," Jane said carefully, "and go back to your room and pack your things and leave, and that would be the end. You'd be another discarded woman who signed an NDA and disappeared. Or —"

"Or what?" Blanche looked at her. Not with hope. Not with gratitude. With something harder, something angular: fury, barely contained, pressing against the inside of her skin like a woman trying to escape her own outline.
"Do you want to help?" Jane asked.

The question landed between them like a stone dropped into still water. Blanche flinched. Her fingers tightened on the cellophane, and it crackled — that cheap, scratchy sound again, impossibly loud in the small space.

"What does helping look like?" Blanche's voice was flat. Not skeptical — genuinely uncertain. She had spent three years being told what to wear and how to stand and where to look and when to smile. The concept of agency was foreign to her now, a language she'd once spoken fluently but had forgotten through disuse.
"Testimony," Jane said. "When it's time, you tell the world what he did. Not just to her — to you."
Blanche's jaw tightened. "They'll say I'm bitter. A discarded woman scrambling for relevance on her way out."
"They'll say that anyway." Jane held her gaze. "At least this way, you're saying it too."

Something moved across Blanche's face — a transformation Jane recognized because she'd felt its equivalent inside herself. The shift from being shaped to choosing. From being the object of the narrative to becoming its author. Blanche Ingram, who had spent three years as Edward Rochester's most beautiful accessory, was being asked — perhaps for the first time — to be a witness. Not a muse. Not an ambassador. Not a mannequin in couture. A witness. Someone whose testimony mattered because it was true, not because it was flattering.

Blanche let go of the gift basket. She set it on the floor beside her, the cellophane settling with a quiet, papery sigh. Then she wiped her face with both hands — rough, unceremonious, the gesture of a woman stripping away a mask — and looked at Jane with eyes that were red-rimmed and swollen and absolutely clear.

"I heard her," Blanche said. "I heard her for two years, and I told myself it was pipes." Her voice broke, but she pushed through it. "I chose not to hear. That's — that's mine. That's on me. I can't undo that." She took a shaking breath. "But I can choose now."

Jane nodded. That was all. She didn't reach for Blanche's hand, didn't offer hollow comfort or performative solidarity. She simply acknowledged what had been said — the full weight of it, the complicity and the cruelty and the fragile, terrifying act of choosing differently after years of choosing not to.

Blanche stood up. She was unsteady — her heels caught on the muslin backing, and she had to brace herself against the shelf — but she stood. She squared her shoulders. The cashmere coat was creased now, the silk blouse stained with mascara and tears, and she looked nothing like the woman in the campaign posters, nothing like the Thornfield ambassador, nothing like a muse. She looked like a person who had just crawled out of the wreckage of her own compliance and was standing upright for the first time in years.

She was still wearing Thornfield. But something had shifted in the way she wore it — the difference between being dressed and being clothed, between being displayed and being present. She walked to the door like a woman choosing to walk, not one being paraded.
She paused with her hand on the doorknob. "Jane."
"Yes."
"If you're wrong about this — if you can't prove what he's done — I'll have nothing. No career, no reputation, no future in this industry. He'll make sure of it."
"I know," Jane said.

Blanche nodded once. She didn't say thank you. This wasn't a kindness; it was a reckoning. She opened the door and walked down the corridor toward her dressing room, her heels clicking on the concrete with a rhythm that sounded — for the first time — like her own.

Jane remained in the closet for another minute. The gift basket sat abandoned on the floor, the scarf spilling out of its tissue paper wrapping, the jacquard pattern catching the overhead light. Cedar and something synthetic — she could smell it from here. She picked up her phone and typed:
*Blanche Ingram. Witness. Not because she saw everything — because she chose not to see, and now she's choosing otherwise.*
She stared at the words for a long moment. Then she saved the note and put the phone away.
That night, at half past eleven, Jane's phone buzzed on the nightstand. A text from an unknown number. The area code was Paris.
*When the time comes, I'll be there. And I won't be wearing Thornfield.*

Jane read the message twice. Then she set the phone face-down on the nightstand and stared at the ceiling of her room at Thornfield, listening to the old house settle around her — the creak of beams, the whisper of the Loire wind against the windows, and somewhere, far below, so faint she might have imagined it, the sound of scissors.

The coalition was growing. But Blanche's testimony would be a double-edged sword — the confession of a woman who had heard and chosen not to hear, who had worn his brand like a second skin and smiled for his cameras while someone suffered beneath her feet. The world would ask why she didn't act sooner. The world would say she was complicit. The world would say she had no right to speak now that she'd been discarded.
But Blanche Ingram was going to speak anyway.
And she would not be wearing Thornfield.
